schools.summary: That this was the lot of the tribe of Judah, the first and chief inheritance divided in the land: 'this then was the lot of the tribe of the children of Judah by their families; even to the border of Edom the wilderness of Zin southward was the uttermost part of the south coast' — the inheritance by lot — 'this then was the lot of the tribe of the children of Judah by their families; even to the border of Edom the wilderness of Zin southward': Judah, the chief tribe, received the first lot in the promised land (the witnesses noting the significance — the division of the inheritance by lot, the portion of each tribe assigned not by man's will but by God's appointment through the lot, Judah first and foremost, the royal tribe, given the first and largest portion, the lot the means by which God allotted the inheritance, the lesson — that the inheritance of God's people is of his appointment, the lot disposing each portion, 'the lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD,' Proverbs 16:33, the truth that God assigns to his people their place and portion, the inheritance his gift and not man's choice, 'the lines are fallen unto me in pleasant places; yea, I have a goodly heritage,' Psalm 16:6, the figure — the portion appointed by God, fulfilled in the inheritance of the saints, 'to give an inheritance among all them which are sanctified,' Acts 20:32, the truth that Judah, the tribe of the promise and of the King, received the chief portion, a figure of the pre-eminence of Christ's kingdom, the figure of the inheritance distributed by God to each according to his will, the call to rest content with the portion God appoints, the assurance that God gives his people a goodly heritage, the figure of the inheritance given by lot, the portion of God's appointment); Judah, the chief tribe, receiving the first lot (the inheritance by lot, Proverbs 16:33) — the lot (of Judah); 'even to the border of Edom the wilderness of Zin southward' (the southern border of Judah's portion reaching to Edom, the inheritance bounded as God allotted, a figure of the portion appointed to the saints, Psalm 16:6; Acts 20:32) — the figure (the inheritance distributed by God to each according to his will, the providence, the lot, the bounds of the south border to follow, vv.2-4) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the lot of the tribe of Judah signify?

sides.position: The inheritance given by lot, the portion of God's appointment - the division of the inheritance by lot, the portion of each tribe assigned not by man's will but by God's disposing (Proverbs 16:33), Judah the chief tribe given the first portion as a goodly heritage (Psalm 16:6), a figure of the inheritance of the saints, each having his place by God's allotting (Acts 20:32)

themes: This then was the lot of the tribe of the children of Judah by their families; even to the border of Edom the wilderness of Zin southward was the uttermost part of the south coast

the division of the inheritance by lot, the portion of each tribe assigned by God's appointment, Judah the chief tribe given the first portion
a figure of the inheritance of the saints, each having his place by God's allotting (Proverbs 16:33; Psalm 16:6; Acts 20:32)

schools.summary: The south border completing at the river of Egypt and the sea: 'from thence it passed toward Azmon, and went out unto the river of Egypt; and the goings out of that coast were at the sea: this shall be your south coast' — the south border reached the river of Egypt and ended at the sea (the from thence it passed toward Azmon, and went out unto the river of Egypt, to the river of Egypt, the and the goings out of that coast were at the sea: this shall be your south coast, ending at the sea, the figure, the south border completed at the sea, the portion fully bounded, the truth that the southern line of Judah was traced to its end, the figure of the inheritance bounded on every side, the portion complete, the south coast defined, the inheritance marked, vv.3); the border to the river of Egypt (the bound) — the river (of Egypt); 'the goings out of that coast were at the sea: this shall be your south coast' (the border ending at the sea, the south coast, the figure, the inheritance bounded on every side, the providence, the coast, the east and north borders to follow, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The east border at the salt sea and the start of the north border: 'and the east border was the salt sea, even unto the end of Jordan. schools.summary: The north border by Bethhogla and the stone of Bohan: 'and the border went up to Bethhogla, and passed along by the north of Betharabah; and the border went up to the stone of Bohan the son of Reuben' — the north border ran up by Bethhogla and Betharabah to the stone of Bohan (the and the border went up to Bethhogla, and passed along by the north of Betharabah, by Bethhogla and Betharabah, the and the border went up to the stone of Bohan the son of Reuben, to the stone of Bohan, the figure, the border traced by its landmarks, the portion bounded, the truth that the north line was marked by known places and the stone of Bohan, the figure of the inheritance bounded by its landmarks, the portion defined, the border by the stone of Bohan, the inheritance marked, vv.5); the border by Bethhogla and Betharabah (the line) — the line (by Bethhogla); 'the border went up to the stone of Bohan the son of Reuben' (the border to the stone of Bohan, the landmark, the figure, the inheritance bounded by its landmarks, the providence, the line, the border to Debir and Gilgal to follow, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The north border by the valley of Achor toward Enrogel: 'and the border went up toward Debir from the valley of Achor, and so northward, looking toward Gilgal, that is before the going up to Adummim, which is on the south side of the river: and the border passed toward the waters of Enshemesh, and the goings out thereof were at Enrogel' — the border ran by the valley of Achor and Enshemesh to Enrogel (the and the border went up toward Debir from the valley of Achor, and so northward, looking toward Gilgal, by the valley of Achor, the and the border passed toward the waters of Enshemesh, and the goings out thereof were at Enrogel, to Enrogel, the figure, the border traced by the valley of Achor, where Achan was judged, to Enrogel, the truth that the line passed the place of Achan's judgment, the figure of the inheritance bounded by its landmarks, even the place of judgment, the border to Enrogel, the inheritance marked, vv.6); the border by the valley of Achor (the line) — the line (the valley of Achor); 'the border passed toward the waters of Enshemesh, and the goings out thereof were at Enrogel' (the border to Enrogel, the bound, the figure, the inheritance bounded by its landmarks, the providence, the line, the border by the valley of Hinnom and Jerusalem to follow, vv.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The north border to Nephtoah and Kirjathjearim: 'and the border was drawn from the top of the hill unto the fountain of the water of Nephtoah, and went out to the cities of mount Ephron; and the border was drawn to Baalah, which is Kirjathjearim' — the border ran to Nephtoah and on to Kirjathjearim (the and the border was drawn from the top of the hill unto the fountain of the water of Nephtoah, to Nephtoah, the and went out to the cities of mount Ephron; and the border was drawn to Baalah, which is Kirjathjearim, to Kirjathjearim, the figure, the border traced to Kirjathjearim, where the ark would later rest, the truth that the line passed the place where the ark would abide, the figure of the inheritance bounded by its landmarks, even the place of the ark, the border to Kirjathjearim, the inheritance marked, vv.8); the border to Nephtoah (the line) — the line (to Nephtoah); 'the border was drawn to Baalah, which is Kirjathjearim' (the border to Kirjathjearim, where the ark would rest, the landmark, the figure, the inheritance bounded by the place of the ark, the providence, the line, the border to Seir and Bethshemesh to follow, vv.10) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The north border by mount Seir to Timnah: 'and the border compassed from Baalah westward unto mount Seir, and passed along unto the side of mount Jearim, which is Chesalon, on the north side, and went down to Bethshemesh, and passed on to Timnah' — the border ran by mount Seir and Bethshemesh to Timnah (the and the border compassed from Baalah westward unto mount Seir, and passed along unto the side of mount Jearim, which is Chesalon, on the north side, by mount Seir, the and went down to Bethshemesh, and passed on to Timnah, to Timnah, the figure, the border traced by mount Seir to Timnah, the portion bounded, the truth that the line passed Bethshemesh and Timnah, places of later history, the figure of the inheritance bounded by its landmarks, the portion defined, the border to Timnah, the inheritance marked, vv.9); the border by mount Seir (the line) — the line (by mount Seir); 'went down to Bethshemesh, and passed on to Timnah' (the border to Bethshemesh and Timnah, the bound, the figure, the inheritance bounded by its landmarks, the providence, the line, the border to Ekron and the sea to follow, vv.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The north border completing at the sea by Ekron: 'and the border went out unto the side of Ekron northward: and the border was drawn to Shicron, and passed along to mount Baalah, and went out unto Jabneel; and the goings out of the border were at the sea' — the north border ran by Ekron and ended at the sea (the and the border went out unto the side of Ekron northward, by Ekron, the and the border was drawn to Shicron, and passed along to mount Baalah, and went out unto Jabneel; and the goings out of the border were at the sea, ending at the sea, the figure, the north border completed at the sea, the portion fully bounded, the truth that the northern line of Judah ended at the great sea, the figure of the inheritance bounded on every side, the portion complete, the border at the sea, the inheritance marked, vv.10); the border by Ekron (the bound) — the bound (by Ekron); 'the goings out of the border were at the sea' (the border ending at the sea, the bound, the figure, the inheritance bounded on every side, the providence, the border, the west border the great sea to follow, vv.12) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The west border, the great sea, and the full compass of Judah's coast: 'and the west border was to the great sea, and the coast thereof. schools.summary: That Caleb received his portion, Hebron, according to the commandment of the LORD: 'and unto Caleb the son of Jephunneh he gave a part among the children of Judah, according to the commandment of the LORD to Joshua, even the city of Arba the father of Anak, which city is Hebron' — the reward of faith — 'and unto Caleb the son of Jephunneh he gave a part among the children of Judah, according to the commandment of the LORD to Joshua, even the city of Arba the father of Anak, which city is Hebron': Caleb received Hebron, the city of Arba the giant, as his portion, given according to God's command, the reward of his faith and full following of the LORD (the witnesses noting the significance — the faithfulness of God in keeping his promise to Caleb, who wholly followed the LORD when the other spies failed, now given his promised inheritance by God's express command, the portion not by lot but by the commandment of the LORD, the reward of the faith that had stood when others quailed, 'because he hath another spirit with him, and hath followed me fully,' Numbers 14:24, the lesson — that God rewards the faith and faithfulness of his servants, keeping his word to them, the promise made forty-five years before now fulfilled, the long-deferred promise kept, the reward of perseverance, 'thy land whereon thy feet have trodden shall be thine inheritance,' Joshua 14:9, the truth that God is faithful to reward those who fully follow him, the promise sure, 'he is faithful that promised,' Hebrews 10:23, the figure — the inheritance given to the faithful, fulfilled in the reward of those who endure and follow Christ fully, 'to him that overcometh will I grant to sit with me,' Revelation 3:21, the truth that the place of the giants, the hardest portion, was given to the man of strongest faith, who feared not the Anakim, the figure of the reward of faith that claims the promise and possesses it, the call to follow the LORD fully and inherit the promise, the assurance that God keeps his word to the faithful, the figure of the reward of faith, the inheritance promised to the faithful and given by God's word); Caleb's portion given not by lot but by the express commandment of the LORD, the reward of his faith and full following (the promise kept, Numbers 14:24; Joshua 14:9) — the portion (by God's command); 'even the city of Arba the father of Anak, which city is Hebron' (Hebron, the city of Arba the giant, given to Caleb, the hardest portion given to the man of strongest faith, a figure of the inheritance of the faithful who overcome, the reward of faith, Hebrews 10:23; Revelation 3:21) — the figure (the reward of those who endure and follow Christ fully, the providence, the gift, Caleb driving out the three sons of Anak to follow, vv.14) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Caleb's portion given by the commandment of the LORD signify?

sides.position: The reward of faith, the inheritance promised to the faithful and given by God's word - the faithfulness of God in keeping his promise to Caleb, who wholly followed the LORD when others failed (Numbers 14:24), the long-deferred promise of forty-five years kept (Joshua 14:9), God faithful to reward those who fully follow him (Hebrews 10:23), the place of the giants given to the man of strongest faith, a figure of the reward of those who overcome and follow Christ fully (Revelation 3:21)

themes: And unto Caleb the son of Jephunneh he gave a part among the children of Judah, according to the commandment of the LORD to Joshua, even the city of Arba the father of Anak, which city is Hebron
that Caleb received his portion, Hebron, according to the commandment of the LORD (the crux of the reward of faith)
the faithfulness of God in keeping his promise to Caleb, who wholly followed the LORD when others failed, the long-deferred promise of forty-five years kept
the place of the giants given to the man of strongest faith, a figure of the reward of those who overcome and follow Christ fully (Numbers 14:24; Joshua 14:9; Hebrews 10:23; Revelation 3:21)

schools.summary: That Caleb drove out the three sons of Anak from Hebron: 'and Caleb drove thence the three sons of Anak, Sheshai, and Ahiman, and Talmai, the children of Anak' — the victory of faith — 'and Caleb drove thence the three sons of Anak, Sheshai, and Ahiman, and Talmai, the children of Anak': Caleb drove out the three giant sons of Anak, the very Anakim whose name had once terrified the spies (the witnesses noting the significance — the triumph of Caleb's faith over the giants, the conquest of the very terror that had made the ten spies and the people quail and turn back, the same Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai, the sons of Anak, that the spies had reported, 'the children of Anak, which come of the giants... we were in our own sight as grasshoppers,' Numbers 13:33, now driven out by the man who had believed God, the lesson — that faith overcomes the very terrors that unbelief flees, Caleb taking the giants' stronghold where unbelief had despaired, 'if God be for us, who can be against us?' Romans 8:31, the truth that the faith that follows God fully conquers what unbelief dreads, the giants no obstacle to the believing, 'this is the victory that overcometh the world, even our faith,' 1 John 5:4, the figure — the conquest of faith over its greatest terrors, the believer overcoming the powers that dismay the faithless, the truth that what the flesh calls impossible, faith possesses, the giants driven out by the old warrior at eighty-five, the figure of the victory of faith that fears no giant and claims the promise, the call to face the terrors of the way in faith, the assurance that faith overcomes the giants, the figure of the victory of faith over the giants, the terrors that unbelief had fled); Caleb driving out the giant sons of Anak from Hebron, the conquest of the giants by the believing warrior (the victory of faith, Numbers 13:33) — the conquest (the sons of Anak driven out); 'Sheshai, and Ahiman, and Talmai, the children of Anak' (the very giants, Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai, whom the spies had feared, now driven out by Caleb, the faith that follows God fully conquering what unbelief dreads, the victory that overcomes, Romans 8:31; 1 John 5:4) — the figure (the conquest of faith over its greatest terrors, the providence, the conquest, Caleb's offer for Kirjathsepher to follow, vv.15-16) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Caleb's driving out the sons of Anak signify?

sides.position: The victory of faith over the giants, the terrors that unbelief had fled - the triumph of Caleb's faith over the very Anakim whose name had made the ten spies and the people quail and turn back (Numbers 13:33), faith overcoming what unbelief flees, the giants no obstacle to the believing (Romans 8:31), the faith that follows God fully conquering what the flesh dreads and overcoming the world (1 John 5:4)

themes: And Caleb drove thence the three sons of Anak, Sheshai, and Ahiman, and Talmai, the children of Anak

schools.summary: Caleb going up against Debir, formerly Kirjathsepher: 'and he went up thence to the inhabitants of Debir: and the name of Debir before was Kirjathsepher' — Caleb advanced from Hebron against Debir, once called Kirjathsepher (the and he went up thence to the inhabitants of Debir, against Debir, the and the name of Debir before was Kirjathsepher, formerly Kirjathsepher, the figure, the further conquest, Caleb pressing on from Hebron to Debir, the truth that Caleb did not rest in one victory but pressed on to take Debir, the figure of the faith that presses on from conquest to conquest, not resting, the advance to Debir, the conquest continued, vv.14); Caleb advancing against Debir (the advance) — the advance (to Debir); 'the name of Debir before was Kirjathsepher' (Debir, once Kirjathsepher, the city, the figure, the faith that presses on from conquest to conquest, the providence, the advance, Caleb's offer of Achsah to follow, vv.16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he went up thence to the inhabitants of Debir: and the name of Debir before was Kirjathsepher

Caleb advancing from Hebron against Debir, once called Kirjathsepher

schools.summary: Caleb's offer of his daughter Achsah to the one who would take Kirjathsepher: 'and Caleb said, He that smiteth Kirjathsepher, and taketh it, to him will I give Achsah my daughter to wife' — the reward of valour — 'and Caleb said, He that smiteth Kirjathsepher, and taketh it, to him will I give Achsah my daughter to wife': Caleb offered his daughter Achsah as the reward to whoever would take the city (the witnesses noting the significance — the prize held out to stir up valour and reward the brave deed, the daughter of the man of faith given to the man of valour, the offer not rash but to encourage the taking of the city, the reward proportioned to the deed, the lesson — that God and his servants hold out reward to encourage faithful and valiant service, the prize given to him who overcomes, 'to him that overcometh will I give to eat of the tree of life,' Revelation 2:7, the truth that the brave deed is honoured and rewarded, the man of valour winning the prize, as the warrior wins the bride, the figure — the reward held out to the faithful soldier, fulfilled in the prize given to those who fight the good fight, 'henceforth there is laid up for me a crown of righteousness,' 2 Timothy 4:8, the truth that the daughter of Caleb, the man of faith, was given to Othniel, the man of valour, the faithful joined to the faithful, the figure of the reward that crowns the overcomer, the bride given to the conqueror, the call to valiant service in hope of the reward, the assurance that the brave deed is not unrewarded, the figure of the reward held out to valour and the prize of faithful service); Caleb offering a reward to whoever would take the city, the prize held out to stir up valour (the reward of valour, Revelation 2:7) — the offer (he that taketh it); 'to him will I give Achsah my daughter to wife' (Achsah given as the prize to the man of valour, the daughter of the man of faith joined to the conqueror, a figure of the reward that crowns the faithful soldier, the prize of service, 2 Timothy 4:8) — the figure (the reward given to those who fight the good fight, the providence, the offer, Othniel taking the city and winning Achsah to follow, vv.17) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Caleb's offer of Achsah for the taking of the city signify?

sides.position: The reward held out to valour and the prize of faithful service - the prize offered to stir up valour and crown the brave deed, the daughter of the man of faith given to the man of valour, the prize given to him who overcomes (Revelation 2:7), the reward that crowns the faithful soldier who fights the good fight (2 Timothy 4:8)

themes: And Caleb said, He that smiteth Kirjathsepher, and taketh it, to him will I give Achsah my daughter to wife
Caleb's offer of his daughter Achsah to the one who would take Kirjathsepher (the crux of the reward held out to valour)
the prize held out to stir up valour and reward the brave deed, the daughter of the man of faith given to the man of valour
the prize given to him who overcomes, a figure of the reward that crowns the faithful soldier (Revelation 2:7; 2 Timothy 4:8)

schools.summary: Othniel taking the city and winning Achsah: 'and Othniel the son of Kenaz, the brother of Caleb, took it: and he gave him Achsah his daughter to wife' — Othniel took Kirjathsepher and won Achsah as his wife (the and Othniel the son of Kenaz, the brother of Caleb, took it, Othniel taking the city, the and he gave him Achsah his daughter to wife, winning Achsah, the figure, Othniel, later the first judge, winning the prize by his valour, the truth that Othniel, the man of valour who would deliver Israel, took the city and the bride, the figure of the conqueror who wins the prize, the reward of the brave deed, the faithful joined to the faithful, Othniel's valour rewarded, the reward won, vv.16); Othniel, later the first judge, taking the city (the valour) — the deed (took it); 'he gave him Achsah his daughter to wife' (Othniel winning Achsah, the reward of his valour, the prize, the figure, the conqueror who wins the prize, the providence, the reward, Achsah's request for a field and springs to follow, vv.18-19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Othniel the son of Kenaz, the brother of Caleb, took it: and he gave him Achsah his daughter to wife
Othniel taking the city and winning Achsah
Othniel, later the first judge of Israel, taking Kirjathsepher by his valour and winning Achsah as his wife
the reward of the brave deed

schools.summary: Achsah moving Othniel to ask a field and alighting before Caleb: 'and it came to pass, as she came unto him, that she moved him to ask of her father a field: and she lighted off her ass; and Caleb said unto her, What wouldest thou?' — Achsah moved Othniel to ask a field, then alighting before her father, was asked what she desired (the and it came to pass, as she came unto him, that she moved him to ask of her father a field, moving Othniel to ask a field, the and she lighted off her ass; and Caleb said unto her, What wouldest thou?, alighting before Caleb, the figure, Achsah's desire for more than the bare portion, the seeking of a fruitful field, the truth that Achsah, not content with the dry land, sought a better portion, the figure of the soul that seeks more of God's blessing, not content with little, 'open thy mouth wide, and I will fill it,' Psalm 81:10, Achsah's request, the seeking of the fuller blessing, the desire for more, vv.19); Achsah moving Othniel to ask a field (the desire, Psalm 81:10) — the request (a field); 'she lighted off her ass; and Caleb said unto her, What wouldest thou?' (Achsah alighting before Caleb, who asked her desire, the asking, the figure, the soul that seeks more of God's blessing, the providence, the request, Achsah's asking for springs of water to follow, vv.19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And it came to pass, as she came unto him, that she moved him to ask of her father a field: and she lighted off her ass; and Caleb said unto her, What wouldest thou?
Achsah moving Othniel to ask a field and alighting before Caleb
Achsah, not content with the bare portion, moving Othniel to ask a field and alighting before her father
the seeking of the fuller blessing (Psalm 81:10)

schools.summary: Achsah's request for a blessing and springs of water, and Caleb's gift of the upper and nether springs: 'who answered, Give me a blessing; for thou hast given me a south land; give me also springs of water. And he gave her the upper springs, and the nether springs' — the asking of the fuller blessing — 'who answered, Give me a blessing; for thou hast given me a south land; give me also springs of water. And he gave her the upper springs, and the nether springs': Achsah, given a dry south land, asked her father for springs of water, and received both the upper and the nether springs (the witnesses noting the significance — the holy boldness of the daughter who asks her father for the fuller blessing, not content with the dry land but seeking the springs that would make it fruitful, and receiving more than she asked, the south land barren without water, the springs the very thing that gives it worth, the father giving both upper and nether springs, abundance beyond the request, the lesson — that the children of God are to ask of their Father the full blessing, the springs of living water, not content with the bare inheritance, and that he gives liberally and beyond their asking, 'ask, and it shall be given you,' Matthew 7:7, the truth that the Father gives good gifts to his children who ask, and gives abundantly, 'able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think,' Ephesians 3:20, the figure — the soul thirsting for the springs of living water, the fuller blessing of the Spirit, 'whosoever drinketh of the water that I shall give him shall never thirst,' John 4:14, the truth that the dry portion is made fruitful by the springs, the blessing of God that waters the inheritance, the figure of the believer who asks for and receives the upper and nether springs, the blessings of heaven and earth, the call to ask boldly for the fuller blessing, the assurance that the Father gives more than we ask, the figure of the soul that asks of the Father the fuller blessing and is not denied); Achsah asking her father for a blessing and springs of water, not content with the dry south land but seeking the springs that would make it fruitful (the asking of the fuller blessing, Matthew 7:7) — the request (springs of water); 'and he gave her the upper springs, and the nether springs' (Caleb giving both the upper and the nether springs, more than she asked, a figure of the Father who gives abundantly above our asking, the springs of living water that water the dry inheritance, the abundant gift, Ephesians 3:20; John 4:14) — the figure (the soul thirsting for the springs of living water, the providence, the gift, the cities of Judah in the south to follow, vv.20-21) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Achsah's request for springs of water signify?

sides.position: The soul that asks of the Father the fuller blessing and is not denied - the holy boldness of the daughter who asks her father for springs, not content with the dry land, and receives more than she asked, the children of God asking the full blessing of their Father who gives liberally and abundantly (Matthew 7:7; Ephesians 3:20), a figure of the soul thirsting for the springs of living water, the fuller blessing of the Spirit (John 4:14)

themes: Who answered, Give me a blessing; for thou hast given me a south land; give me also springs of water. And he gave her the upper springs, and the nether springs
Achsah's request for a blessing and springs of water, and Caleb's gift of the upper and nether springs (the crux of the soul that asks of the Father the fuller blessing)
the holy boldness of the daughter who asks her father for the fuller blessing, not content with the dry land but seeking the springs, and receiving more than she asked
a figure of the soul thirsting for the springs of living water, the Father who gives abundantly above our asking (Matthew 7:7; Ephesians 3:20; John 4:14)
